coredata.dc:description: Exploring food packaging films simultaneously possess freshness monitoring and maintaining can effectively tackle food safety issues. Here, we constructed a chitosan/N-doped carbon dots (CS/N-CDs) film which can monitor the freshness of pork based on pH-mediated fluorescent sensing and extend the shelf life based on the antioxidant, antibacterial and UV shielding properties. The fluorescent intensity of CS/N-CDs films increased with the increase of pork pH from 5.77 to 6.84 which positively related to the change of TVB-N from 6.68 to 17.53 mg/100 g. CS/N-CDs films had a maintaining effect on pork freshness corroborated by the scavenging activity on DPPH and ABTS radicals and antibacterial efficiency and inhibition zones of S. aureus and E.coli, and the total viable count, a value, weight loss, hardness and springiness of pork. Cell viability and hemolytic activity assay proved that CS/N-CDs films was safe. A novel chitosan-based intelligent and active food packaging film was provided.

head.ce:title: A dual-function chitosan packaging film for simultaneously monitoring and maintaining pork freshness

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring food packaging films simultaneously possess freshness monitoring and maintaining can effectively tackle food safety issues. Here, we constructed a chitosan/N-doped carbon dots (CS/N-CDs) film which can monitor the freshness of pork based on pH-mediated fluorescent sensing and extend the shelf life based on the antioxidant, antibacterial and UV shielding properties. The fluorescent intensity of CS/N-CDs films increased with the increase of pork pH from 5.77 to 6.84 which positively related to the change of TVB-N from 6.68 to 17.53 mg/100 g. CS/N-CDs films had a maintaining effect on pork freshness corroborated by the scavenging activity on DPPH and ABTS radicals and antibacterial efficiency and inhibition zones of S. aureus and E.coli, and the total viable count, a value, weight loss, hardness and springiness of pork. Cell viability and hemolytic activity assay proved that CS/N-CDs films was safe. A novel chitosan-based intelligent and active food packaging film was provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage has caused a series of food safety and food waste issues. Exploring novel multifunctional food packaging is one of the effective approaches to tackle these problems. Smart packaging and active packaging are the main types of functional packaging. Intelligent food packaging that can offer real-time food freshness information for consumers based on sensing, detection and recording of internal and external changes in the food products is convincing. The pH indicator packaging is one of the most popular intelligent packaging by monitoring decay information through the change of pH. Once protein-rich food is spoiled, organic amines will be released, resulting in the change of pH of the surrounding environment. For instance, Wen et al. providing a food packaging film containing pH sensitive anthocyanin-rich purple potato extract for shrimp freshness real-time monitoring by the color change of the films from pink to purple (Wen et al., 2021). Unfortunately, these colorimetric packaging are susceptible to the color of the food matrix, which will affect the accurate monitoring of food freshness. Active food packaging plays a significant role in preventing food quality deterioration because it can extend shelf life by the release of the incorporated active compounds that owns special functions such as antioxidant, antibacterial, etc, and by absorbing substances from the packaged food (Jiang et al., 2021; Lin, Ni, & Pang, 2020; Su et al., 2021). However, the lack of monitoring on the food quality will cause imbalance between additive amount of the active substances and demand of food for additive amount of the active substances, further resulting in insufficient or excessive addition of active substances. Also, using active packaging films will may still lead to safety issues because active substances may sudden release or be inactivated due to the changeable external environment. Therefore, exploring food packaging simultaneously possess intelligent and active functions is an effective approach to address food safety and food waste issues.

Recently, natural biodegradable polysaccharides such as cellulose (Wen et al., 2021), chitosan (Jayakumar, & Prabaharan, 2021; Su et al., 2021; Sun, Jiang, Wu, Tong, Pang, & Wu, 2020; Yadav, Mehrotra, Bhartiya, Singh, & Dutt, 2020; Yadav, Mehrotra, & Dutta, 2021), starch (Mousavi, Daneshvar, Seyed Dorraji, Ghasempour, Panahi-Azar, & Ehsani, 2021) and konjac glucomannan (Lin, Ni, & Pang, 2019; Sun, Jiang, Li, et al., 2020) have been prevalently used in preparing food packaging films as the petroleum-based materials will result in white pollution. Among them, chitosan (CS), a liner polysaccharide consisting of (1,4)-linked 2-amino-deoxy-b-d-glucan, is derived by deacetylation of chitin that can be found in the shells of marine arthropods and insects and the cell walls of fungi and algae, etc. It is a natural polycationic with good film-forming property, antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, low gas permeability and rich source property (Hsan, Dutta, Kumar, Bera, & Das, 2019). However, pristine CS films has limited antibacterial and antioxidant activities, which is difficult for effective food storage. Thus, numerous studies have been focused on improving the antibacterial and antioxidant activities of CS-based food packaging films by the incorporation of active substances such as phenolic-rich extracts (Eze, Jayeoye, & Singh, 2022), peel extracts (Jiang et al., 2021), sulfur nanoparticles (Shankar & Rhim, 2018), etc.

Herein, as shown in Scheme 1 , an intelligent and active food packaging film simultaneously possesses freshness monitoring and maintaining was constructed by incorporating N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) into CS film matrix. CDs, a 0D material with fluorescent properties, have attracted extensive interest for various applications due to their excellent properties such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent photostability and chemical stability, low cost, etc (Zheng et al., 2013). More importantly, it also has fluorescent pH-responsive properties (Bogireddy, Lara, Fragoso, & Agarwal, 2020), antioxidant and antibacterial activities (Das, Ganguly, Margel, & Gedanken, 2021). Through this careful design, on the one hand, the influence of food matrix on the quality monitoring can be effectively avoided by the fluorescent pH-responsive properties of films because the acquisition of fluorescent signals was under the excitation of invisible light, on the other hand, the antibacterial and antioxidant activities of pristine CS films can be enhanced. Besides, pork was chosen as a typical model in the present work to evaluate active and intelligent functions of the films. The fluorescent pH-responsive properties, antioxidant and antibacterial activities of films were determined. The morphology, UV–vis spectra, hydrophobicity, water and oxygen barrier properties, surface color and mechanical properties of CS/N-CDs film were also studied. Furthermore, the physical and chemical indicators of the pork packaged by CS/N-CDs film including weight, hardness, springiness, pH value, surface color, total volatile bases nitrogen and total viable counts were recorded during storage. Finally, the biotoxicity of the pork packed by CS/N-CDs film were evaluated by the cell viability and hemolytic activity assay.

Chitosan (CAS number: 9012–76-4, degree of deacetylation: 80.0 ∼ 95.0%), glycerol (CAS number: 56–81-5) and KBr (CAS number: 7758–02-3) were gained from National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid monohydrate (CAS number: 5949–29-1, CA), urea (CAS number: 57–13-6), H3BO3 (CAS number: 10043–35-3), H3PO4 (CAS number: 7664–38-2), NaOH (CAS number: 1310–73-2), CaCl2 (CAS number: 10043–52-4) and NaCl (CAS number: 7647–14-5) were purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Acetic acid (CAS number: 64–19-7) was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). HCl (CAS number: 7647–01-0) was supplied by Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline −6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (CAS number: 30931–67-0), 2,2-Di(4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (CAS number: 84077–81-6), 95% ethanol (CAS number: 64–17-5) and trichloroacetic acid (CAS number: 76–03-9) were acquired from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used without further purification and deionized water (DW) was used in preparing solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of and characterizations N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized according to our previous study with slight modifications (He et al., 2020). 4.0 g citric acid monohydrate and 6.0 g urea were accurately weighed, added into 40 mL of DW followed by stirring and sonication. Then, the obtained homogeneous solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooled at room temperature, a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone (PES) membrane was used to filter the resultant suspension to remove the large particles. Finally, the N-CDs solution was washed by DW and stored at 4 °C for further use. The corresponding N-CDs particles were obtained by freeze-drying under vacuum.

The microstructure of N-CDs was acquired on a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL, Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The functional groups of N-CDs was obtained by a Fourier Transform infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) (Vetex70, Bruker Corp, Germany). The N-CDs solid was mixed with KBr and grained into powder, then compressed to a flake for test with a scanning speed of 4 cm−1 and a wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-CDs was obtained using X-ray diffractometer (SmartLab, Rigaku Corporation, Japan). The Cu-Kα radiation was applied to record the XRD graphs between the 2ϴ angles of 10° and 80° with scan rate of 10°/min.

The fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs were recorded under excitation wavelength of 347 nm to 360 nm by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F98, Shanghai Lingguang Technology Co., Ltd., China) in order to determine the optimal excitation and emission wavelength. Then the FL excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of N-CDs were recorded in the range of 275 nm ∼ 600 nm. As for pH sensing, N-CDs were adjusted to different pH values (pH 4.0, pH 5.0, pH 6.0, pH 7.0 and pH 8.0) using Britton-Robinson (Br) buffer solution with different concentrations of 12.5 μg mL−1, 6.25 μg mL−1, 3.125 μg mL−1 and 2.5 μg mL−1 for pH sensing experiment. Photoluminescence measurements were carried out under optimal excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of CS/N-CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: First, CS was added into 1% of aqueous acetic acid solution (v/v) to obtain a 2 wt% homogeneous CS solution, then the solution was mechanically stirred at room temperature for 6 h followed by another stirring for 2 h after adding with 0.8% glycerol. Secondly, the N-CDs particles were physically mixed with CS solution to acquire film forming solutions (FFS) with different incorporation ratio of 0.1%, 0.5% and 2.5% (w/w: N-CDs: CS), respectively. FFS was ultrasonic treated for 30 min and then was poured into petri dishes with diameter of 9 cm, and kept in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain CS/N-CDs films, labeled as 0.1% CS/N-CDs films, 0.5% CS/N-CDs films and 2.5% CS/N-CDs films, respectively. Finally, the films were placed in a close environment with relatively humidity of 48 ± 2% and 25 ± 2 °C for equilibration before the following characterization and evaluation.
The rheology properties of FFS, fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–visible absorbance spectroscopy of films were analyzed to determine the optimal incorporation of N-CDs into CS film matrix.

The rheology analysis of FFS was carried out by a rotational rheometer (DHR-1, TA Instruments, USA) using a parallel aluminum plate with 40 mm diameter at 25 °C. The shear rate was set from 0.1 to 100 1/s. The sample was placed in the central of parallel plates with the gap height of 1 mm and the equilibration time was set to 120 s. The CS/N-CDs films with different N-CDs incorporation ratio of 0.1%, 0.5% and 2.5% were immersed into various Br buffer solutions of pH 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0 and 7.5 respectively. Then their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The excitation wavelength was the optimal one obtained in Section 2.2 and the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained in the range of 400 nm to 600 nm. The light absorption spectrum of the films was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (N6000, Shanghai Youke Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

The functional groups and morphology of CS/N-CDs films with optimal incorporating ratio of N-CDs were characterized via FTIR and SEM. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CD/N-CDs film was analyzed. Also, the water contact angle (WCA), water vapor permeability (WVP), surface color, mechanical properties, antibacterial and antioxidant activities of films were evaluated.

For FTIR, the infrared region was set from 4000 cm−1 to 1000 cm−1 the same to section 2.2. The cross-section morphology of the films was studied using SEM. The micrographs were collected at accelerating voltage of 10 kV with a magnification of 2.5 k.
For XRD, the data were obtained according to the method described in Section 2.2.

WCA of films were measured by using an angle measuring instrument (SZ-CAMB3, Shanghai Sunzern Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The WCA value was obtained by automatic model calculation. Each sample was carried out 6 times in parallel. 2 μL of distilled water droplet was carefully deposited onto the surface of film, and the angles were measured at three different regions of each sample and averaged. WVP were tested according to our previous method (Lin et al., 2019).

Oxygen vapor permeability of films were measured according to the method reported by Kurt et al. with slight modifications (Kurt, & Kahyaoglu, 2014). 5 mL of sunflower seed oil was poured into 25 mL conical flask and then the flask was sealed with films, and stored at 60 °C for a week. The group without seal treatment was labeled as the control. The peroxide value of the sunflower oil was measured by sodium thiosulfate titration according to People's Republic of China GB 5009.227–2016 (2016). The peroxide value was calculated by Eq. (1):

Mechanical properties of films were tested by using a TA.XT PLUS/50 texture analyzer (Stable Micro Systems, UK) with a load cell of 500 N according to the reported method (Lin et al., 2020).

The antibacterial activity of N-CDs films and CS/N-CDs films were evaluated by measuring inhibition zones and antibacterial efficiency according to the previous method (Lin et al., 2019). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were chosen as the representative Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively. The antibacterial efficiency was calculated by Eq. (3):

The antioxidant activity of N-CDs solution, N-CDs films and CS/N-CDs films were assessed by means of scavenging assay on 2,2-Di(4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline −6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS) free radicals. 50 μg/mL ascorbic acid was used as the positive control. 50 mg of film samples were immersed into 8 mL of ethanol for the following test. For DPPH radical scavenging activity, the test was performed according to the method described by Liu et al (Liu et al., 2015), with slight modifications. 3 mL of film solution or 50 μg/mL N-CDs was incubated with 3 mL of DPPH ethanol solution (0.1 mM) in the dark at 30 °C for 30 min and the absorbance of mixture was measured at 517 nm. The DPPH radical scavenging activity was calculated by Eq. (4):

For ABTS analysis of the antioxidant activity, the test was performed according to the method described by Li et al (Li, Yan, Guan, & Huang, 2020), with slight modifications. 7 mM ABTS and 2.45 mM potassium per sulphate solution were mixed and then stand in dark at room temperature for 12 to 16 h to obtain ABTS•+. The prepared solution was diluted with ethanol to reach an absorbance of 0.7 at 734 nm. Afterwards, 0.2 mL of sample solution was incubated with 4 mL of diluted ABTS•+ in dark at room temperature for 6 min. The ABTS scavenging rate was calculated by Eq. (5):

The CS/N-CDs films with the optimal incorporation was used for packaging test. The sample used in current study was the pork tenderloin purchased from local Hao You Duo supermarket (Yangling, Shaanxi, China). The pork with uniform color was selected and cut into cuboid with uniform size then placed in the glass culture dish with CS/N-CDs films wrapped on the surface. During the packaging test, the pH, total volatile bases nitrogen (TVB-N), total viable counts (TVC), a value, weight, hardness and springiness of the pork were measured on day 1, day 3, day 5, day 7 and day 9, respectively. The pork without packaging was used as the negative control. The pork packed with CS and CS/N-CDs films were the treatment group. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CS/N-CDs films were recorded on the corresponding day.

The a value of pork was measured by the colorimeter according to the method provided in the section 2.3. The weight loss percentage of pork was determined by weighing the samples at specific time intervals compared to initial weight and reported as % weight loss. The harness and springiness were determined by the texture analyzer with a spherical probe (P/0.75S). The crosshead speed and distance were 5 mm/s and 2 mm, respectively. The pH value, TVB-N and TVC were determined according to the method described by National Standard of the People's Republic of China GB/T 9695.5–2008/ISO 2917: 1999, GB 5009.228–2016 (2016) and GB 4789.2–2016, respectively.

The in vitro cytotoxicity assay and hemolytic activity test were applied in evaluating the safety of CS/N-CDs films. The cytotoxicity of the pork packed by films were evaluated by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltertrazolium bromide (MTT) assay using mouse embryonic fibroblast cell line (NIH-3 T3) (Liu et al., 2020). In brief, the culture medium was prepared according to the following formula: 80 mL of DMEM high sugar medium, 15 mL of serum, 1 mL of double antibodies (streptomycin, penicillin) and 1 mL of non-essential amino acids. The mixture was mixed thoroughly under aseptic conditions and store at 4 °C for later use. Then, the NIH-3 T3 was taken out from liquid nitrogen tank to be activated at 37 °C under 5% carbon dioxide atmosphere. After the cells have grown to the logarithmic phase, the cell suspension was sealed in a 96-well plate (each well injected 200 μL), being cultured for 24 h. After that, 100 μL of culture medium was replaced by the sample solutions that pretreated with UV sterilization. After incubation for another 4 h, the culture medium was removed, and 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added into each well. After incubation for 4 h, the medium was discarded, and 100 μL of DMSO was used to dissolve the crystals for 15 mins. Finally, the absorbance was recoded at 492 nm. The NIT-3 T3 without sample solution treatment were labeled as the positive control (PC), and the blank wells without cells were labeled as the negative control (NC). The cell viability was calculated by Eq. (6):

Hemolysis test was carried out according to the reported method (Ni et al., 2021). The fresh blood cells obtained from mouse were washed with PBS solution (0.02 M, pH 7.4), then was redissolved in PBS to prepare the stock dispersion. Sample solution was prepared and then mixed with the stock dispersion at 37 °C for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance was recorded at 492 nm. Stock dispersion incubated with ultrapure water and PBS were labeled as the positive control and the negative control, respectively. Relative hemolysis ratio was calculated by Eq. (7):

All experiments were carried out in triplicate. Statistical analysis was performed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using SPSS statistical program (SPSS 22, SPSS Inc., IBM, New York).

Fig. 1 showed the morphology, functional groups and fluorescent properties of N-CDs. The morphology of the as prepared N-CDs was characterized by TEM shown in Fig. 1A and the corresponding size distribution was analyzed.

TEM images showed that N-CDs were well dispersed, spherical in shape with uniform size. The histogram of size distribution showed diameters ranging between 4.2 nm and 5.0 nm with an average diameter of 4.3 nm. In the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1B), the wide peak at 3200 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibrations of OH/NH (Jiang et al., 2021). The spectral band at 1670 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration (Eze, Jayeoye, & Singh, 2022). Moreover, the peak at 1568 cm−1 could be assigned for stretch vibrations of NH. These results confirmed the presence of –NH2 and –COOH on the prepared N-CDs. These abundant polar functional groups endowed N-CDs with high hydrophilicity in aqueous solution, which could enhance their dispersion in CS matrix.

The influence of excitation wavelength on the emission intensity was shown in Fig. 1C. When the N-CDs were illuminated with different λex (347–360 nm), the λem of N-CDs remained at 441 nm, which indicated that N-CDs was λex-independent and the center of the peak in the emission spectrum was 441 nm. The inset in Fig. 1C was the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs at 441 nm with different λex. The line chart showed an upward trend in the range of 347–354 nm while a downward trend in the range of 354–360 nm, which suggested the optimal λex was 354 nm. The corresponding excitation spectrum can be found in Fig. 1D (red line). The synthesized N-CDs was yellowish under visible light, while it showed intense blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1D), which was consistent with the emission spectrum shown in Fig. 1D (black line). The FL intensity of N-CDs with different concentrations excited at 354 nm at wavelength of 441 nm (with pH ranges from 4.0 to 8.0) were shown in Fig. S1. All curves presented upward trends with the increase of pH value in the range of 4.0 to 8.0 and the data could be fitted into a non-liner curve, which indicated the potentials of N-CDs in sensing pH. The change in the intensity could be resulted from the protonation and deprotonation of the various amino and carboxyl groups in the surface of the N-CDs, thus disrupting the surface charge and its emissivity (Baragau et al., 2021). In addition, Bogireddy et al. also reported a pH-responsive CDs by using citric acid and urea (w/w = 1: 19) as the precursors and the FL intensity also increased by the pH values ranges from 3.0 to 9.0 but with a liner relationship (Bogireddy, Lara, Fragoso, & Agarwal, 2020). For our work, the pH response range can cover the pH value during the spoilage of pork (Ezati, Bang, & Rhim, 2021; Wu, Zhou, Pang, Deng, Xu, & Wang, 2019). Thus, N-CDs was incorporated into CS matrix to prepare pH-responsive fluorescent films for monitoring pork freshness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal incorporation ratio of N-CDs into film matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Rheological measurement was used to illustrate the effects of N-CDs had on the viscoelastic properties of FFS. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the apparent viscosity of CS and FFS decreased with the increase of the shear rate, indicating typical non-Newtonian fluids with pseudoplastic or shear-thinning behavior, which is the characteristic of polysaccharide solutions with high molecular weight. The apparent viscosity-shear rate profiles of FFS depended on the incorporation ratio of N-CDs and the 0.1% CS/N-CDs had the lowest viscosity. It can be clearly seen that the apparent viscosity of FFS were all lower than that of the CS, which indicated their potentials in preparing food packaging films as relatively high viscosity is difficult for dispersing ingredients and eliminating visible air bubbles in the process of preparing films (Ma, Du, Yang, & Wang, 2017).

As presented in Fig. S2, the FL intensity of N-CDs decreased with the increasing concentration, which was a typical aggregation-caused quenching behavior. Therefore, the FL emission spectra of CS/CDs films with different incorporation ratio of N-CDs was recorded (Fig. 2 B). The pH range was set from 5.5 to 7.5 as the variation of pork pH was usually within this range during storage (Ezati, Bang, & Rhim, 2021; Wu et al., 2019). The emission intensity of all CS/N-CDs films showed an upward trend with the increasing pH, which was consistent with the result of optical pH sensing in Fig. S1. It was noticeable that the CS/N-CDs films with the lowest incorporation ratio (0.1%) presented the highest emission intensity, and there was a negative correlation between the emission intensity and loading proportion of N-CDs in film matrix, which was contrary to the result of N-CDs solution in Fig. S1. The decrease FL intensity in 2.5% CS/N-CDs films could be resulted from the aggregation of N-CDs. Besides, there was no significant difference between the FL intensity of 0.1% CS/N-CDs and 0.5% CS/N-CDs films.

UV–vis blocking is a significant feature to manifest the role of packaging films as a protective agent play in lessening the damage of light on food deterioration. As shown in Fig. 2C, all CS/N-CDs films exhibited low transmission values of 45%, 39% and 34%, respectively at 280 nm, signifying their certain ability to block UV compared with pristine CS films. Besides, it was observed that the CS and CS/N-CDs films with N-CDs incorporation ratio of 0.1% and 0.5% showed relatively higher transmission values of 91% at 600 nm compared with that of 2.5% CS/N-CDs films, which was due to the light scattering induced by the filler in the polymer matrix (Terzioğlu, Güney, Parın, Şen, & Tuna, 2021). Higher transparency is more conductive to improving the convenience for consumers to choose products. Therefore, combining the results of the above rheological and fluorescent spectral analysis, 0.5% CS/N-CDs films was chosen for the following characterizations and performance evaluations.

As shown in Fig. 2D, the spectral pattern of CS/N-CDs films was similar to that of the pristine CS films and there was unnoticeable change in the functional groups, denoting that the incorporated N-CDs did not change the chemical structure of the CS. The peaks at around 3151–3558 cm−1 could be assigned for the stretching vibrations of OH/NH in both CS and CS/N-CDs films (Jiang et al., 2021). It was observed that the peaks became deeper in CS/N-CDs films compared to those of CS films, indicating the increased of amine groups, hydroxyl groups, and hydrogen bond interactions in the composite films (Huang et al., 2021). Also, the CS bands at 3458 cm−1 and 3151 cm−1 shifted to 3558 cm−1 and 3167 cm−1 in the composite films, respectively, which implied the formation of hydrogen bonds between CS and N-CDs (Eze, Jayeoye, & Singh, 2022). The corresponding schematic formation of hydrogen bonds in the CS/N-CDs films was shown in Fig. 2E. The CO of the acetyl group (amide I) could be assigned for stretches at 1645 cm−1 in CS and at 1654 cm−1 in CS/N-CDs (Eze, Jayeoye, & Singh, 2022). These results inferred the N-CDs were successfully incorporated into CS matrix and hydrogen bonds were formed between CS and N-CDs.

Fig. 2F and G provided the cross-section morphology and the corresponding macro photos of CS and CS/N-CDs films. Both CS films and CS/N-CDs films showed high transparency and the CS/N-CDs films was yellowish, which was consistent with the result of UV–vis spectra. The average thickness of CS and CS/N-CDs films were 12.4 ± 0.5 μm and 15.1 ± 0.5 μm, respectively. The increased thickness was due to the incorporated N-CDs. The cross section of CS exhibited a relatively rough surface compared with that of CS/N-CDs films, which was ascribe to the incorporated N-CDs. The denser structure was due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CS and N-CDs (Lin et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2017), as confirmed by the FTIR analysis. There were also no obvious aggregation of nanoparticles or loose structure founded in the film matrix, signifying the good compatibility between CS and N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the crystalline nature of the film was evaluated by XRD spectroscopy. CS/N-CDs film displayed diffraction peak at 20.75° (120), which was due to the semi-crystalline nature of CS (Zhang, Liu, Yong, Qin, Liu, & Jin, 2020). The diffraction peak of N-CDs centered at around 23° corresponded to the (001) planes of crystal phase (PDF#87–0657), which demonstrated the graphite structure of N-CDs. The incorporation of N-CDs into CS film matrix did not bring new peak, which may indicate that N-CDs and CS have good compatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water and oxygen barrier, color and mechanical properties
ce:section.ce:para: WCA is a key indicator for evaluating hydrophobicity and surface wettability of film surface (Leceta, Guerrero, & de la Caba, 2013). Here, WCA of CS and CS/N-CDs films were shown in Fig. 3 A. Quantitative definition of the relative terms ‘hydrophobic’ and ‘hydrophilic’ was realized for the first time in 1998 when biomaterial surfaces with WCA below 65° were considered as hydrophilic surfaces (Vogler, 1998). Pristine CS films showed a WCA of 100.6°, illustrating the hydrophobic nature, which was consistent with previous CS films (Su et al., 2021). It was notable that the WCA of all composite films were lower than that of CS, which was due to the presence of hydrophilic amino and carboxyl groups in N-CDs impaired the hydrophobic surface of CS. Nevertheless, the composite films with contact angle ＞ 65° still presented hydrophobic behavior, signifying their potentials in preparing food packaging films. There was no significant difference on WCA between CS and those composite films with pH range of 6.5, 7.0 and 7.5 (97.7°, 98.3° and 96.6°, respectively). However, the rest of composite films had lower WCA than that of CS. This may be resulted from the partial dissolution of CS through protonation in a relatively acidic environment (Leceta, Guerrero, & de la Caba, 2013). These results demonstrated that CS/N-CDs films with hydrophobic nature were applicable in fabricating food packaging materials.

WVP is a crucial indicator of food packaging for evaluating the transfer of moisture between the surrounding atmosphere and the food. Lower WVP is desirable for packaging films as it is beneficial for preventing water vapor from passing through the films to contact with food thereby extend shelf life. There are two commonly used methods to obtain lower WVP. One is to introduce hydrophobic fillers to the film matrix. Other way relies on the formation of dense inner microstructures to reduce the free volume in the films and further decrease the passage of water vapor. Fig. 3B showed WVP data pertaining to the CS and CS/N-CDs films. The WVP of composite films immersed in different buffer were 2.24 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1, 1.96 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1, 1.92 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1, 1.83 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1, 1.50 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1 and 1.54 × 10-3 g mm (m2 h kPa)-1, respectively, which were higher than that of CS, meaning their relatively poor moisture resistance. Though the formation of hydrogen bonds between CS and N-CDs could extend the passage of water vapor inside the films, the hydrophilic N-CDs that has affinity with water vapor would increase the WVP. Moreover, the possible partial dissolution of CS in relative acidic environment may destroy the network structure in the film matrix thereby increasing the WVP. Nevertheless, the WVP of resultant CS/N-CDs films is acceptable compared with recently reported packaging films (Tian, Xu, Cheng, & Liu, 2021). In addition, the WVP measurements lent further supports to the WCA results.

Oxygen vapor permeability of the films was measured to evaluate the oxygen barrier ability of films. Peroxide value (PV) of oil was determined because the oxygen only passes through the films. The lower PV, the higher oxygen barrier. The PV of control, CS film and CS/N-CDs film were 49.7 ± 1.3 meq/kg, 27.0 ± 1.7 meq/kg and 22.3 ± 0.2 meq/kg, respectively. It was noted that the PV of CS/N-CDs film was significantly lower than that of CS film, which signified the improved oxygen barrier property. This may be ascribed to the more compact structure of CS/N-CDs film as hydrogen bonds were formed in the films (Xie, Pan, & Cai, 2022), which was confirmed by the FT-IR analysis. CS/N-CDs film also showed a denser structure in the SEM images.

Film surface color and transparency are essential parameters of food packaging because they can influence the appearance of food and further affect the choice of consumers. As shown in Fig. 3D, all films displayed high L values, signifying their high transparency. The addition of N-CDs led to a significant decrease in lightness and an increase in redness of CS/N-CDs films compared to CS films, which may be beneficial for preventing oxidative deterioration of packaged foods caused by the exposure to ultraviolet and visible light (Rubilar, Cruz, Silva, Vicente, Khmelinskii, & Vieira, 2013). The yellowness of CS/N-CDs films increased, which was in accordance with the inset photos in Fig. 2C. This phenomenon was due to the incorporation of N-CDs because the photo of N-CDs solution taken under visible light was yellowish. Similar observations were made in relation to the whiteness index (WI) and yellowness index (YI) values. Besides, there was no significant difference in surface color between the CS/N-CDs films according to ΔE. Thus, the CS/N-CDs films has no influence on the appearance of food and is suitable for packaging.

The mechanical properties of films are primarily associated with the distribution and density of intermolecular and intramolecular interactions between the polymer chains in the film matrix (Shankar & Rhim, 2018). From Fig. 3C, it could be observed that the tensile strength (TS) of CS/N-CDs-pH7.0 (29.3 ± 1.3 MPa) and CS/N-CDs-pH7.5 (26 ± 0.4 MPa) films were significantly higher than that of CS (17.4 ± 1.0 MPa), which was assigned for their dense structure formed by hydrogen bonds between CS and N-CDs, as confirmed by FTIR analysis. However, the TS of CS/N-CDs-pH5.0 and CS/N-CDs-pH5.5 films decreased down to 19.5 ± 0.9 MPa and 15.1 ± 1.0 MPa, respectively, which may be resulted from the partial dissolution of CS through protonation in a relatively acidic environment (Leceta, Guerrero, & de la Caba, 2013). This might reduce the intermolecular and intramolecular interactions between CS chains or between CS and N-CDs, further affecting the formation of hydrogen bonds thereby disrupting the homogeneity and compactness of the film network. Besides, composite films performed higher EB than that of CS, which may be attributed to the more water content in the CS/N-CDs films. The WVP result showed that CS/N-CDs films had poor water resistant, thus we could deduce that there was more water inside the films than that of CS film. This was consistent with the previous work that moisture can act as a plasticizer in films, and lower water content will reduce the plasticity of films (Li et al., 2021).

Evaluation on DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging ability were used to determine the antioxidant activity. DPPH has nitrogen atoms with lone-pair electrons surrounded by three benzene rings. It can accept the H- radical from antioxidant to form a DPPH-H complex then change color from deep violet to light yellow (Pyrzynska & Pękal, 2013). The glaucous ABTS radicals were formed by the reaction of ABTS and potassium per sulphate, which had a peak absorbance at 734 nm. The antioxidant can react with the ABTS radicals, decreasing the absorbance. As presented in Fig. S4, the activity of N-CDs on eliminating DPPH and ABTS radicals were 24.92% and 32.8%, respectively, which indicated that the synthesized N-CDs have certain antioxidant activity. This result is consistent with the reported study about carbon dots with antioxidant capacity (Das et al., 2021). As presented in Fig. 4 A and B, the pristine CS films showed 8.81% and 12.06% of antioxidant activity in scavenging DPPH and ABTS radicals, respectively. The probable mechanism for the antioxidant of CS was the formation of stable macromolecular radicals and ammonium groups between the residual free amino groups from chitosan and DPPH or ABTS free radicals (Zheng et al., 2019). CS/N-CDs films exhibited DPPH and ABTS radicals scavenging activity of 17.31% and 23.17%, respectively. which was superior to that of CS films. This may ascribe to the synergistic antioxidant effect of N-CDs and CS. The result further illustrated the incorporation of N-CDs could improve the antioxidant activity of CS film.

The antibacterial performance of CS and CS/N-CDs films were investigated by measuring inhibition zones and antibacterial efficiency using S. aureus and E. coli because they are typical food pathogenic microbes. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the inhibition diameters of CS and CS/N-CDs films were 5.5 ± 0.8 mm and 12.1 ± 0.9 mm against S. aureus, respectively, and 4.4 ± 0.6 mm and 10.3 ± 0.6 mm against E.coli, respectively. The corresponding pictures of inhibition zones were shown in Fig. S7. And the antibacterial efficiency were 21% and 31% against S. aureus, respectively, and 15% and 28% against E.coli, respectively. It is noticeable that the CS/N-CDs films exhibited more pronounced antibacterial activity than that of CS films. These results indicated the both CS and N-CDs have effective antibacterial performance towards S. aureus and E.coli and the incorporated N-CDs could improve the antibacterial activity of CS film. The mechanism for the antibacterial activity of CS resulted from the interactions between amino group from CS and negatively charged membrane of microorganisms, which could lead to cell damages. It is speculated that the antibacterial activity of CDs is partially attributed to their damage to the membrane structure of bacteria (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020). Besides, the antibacterial effect of films on Gram-positive bacteria is stronger than that on Gram-negative bacteria, which was due to the different cell walls. Gram-negative bacteria have multilayer structure that contains thin peptidoglycan layer, phospholipids, lipoprotein layer or lipopolysaccharide layer while the gram-positive bacteria only had single peptidoglycan layer.

CS/N-CDs films exhibited pH fluorescent responsive properties, thus the pH value of pork and the FL intensity of CS/N-CDs films used for wrapping pork were recorded during storage. As presented in Fig. 5 A and B, the pH increased with the storage time, which was caused by the decomposition of proteins and accumulation of amines, and the FL intensity of CS/N-CDs films gradually increased from day 1 to day 9. The pH responsive fluorescence property of CS/N-CDs films was conferred by N-CDs. The decomposition of protein in pork was determined by measuring TVB-N that is a volatile nitrogen-containing substance produced by protein decomposition. As shown in Fig. 5C, the TVB-N in CS/N-CDs group gradually increased with prolonged storage time. It should be noted that the pH changes were positively related with TVB-N changes among all group. For maintaining effect of CS/N-CDs films, the TVB-N value of control group was always the highest among all groups and the value had exceeded the limitation of the National Standard of the People's Republic of China GB 2707–2016 (15 mg/100 g) (2016) less than 4 days, whereas the TVB-N value of CS and CS/N-CDs group exceeded the limitation on day 5 and day 8, respectively. This indicated that the CS/N-CDs films could delay the production of TVB-N and thus proved they are preferable for delaying the proteins decomposition of pork. Besides, the change of TVC in pork samples was recorded and the result was shown in Fig. 5D. According to GB/T 9959.2–2008, the TVC for pork was not allowed to exceed 6.0 lg CFU/g. The TVC for fresh pork was 3.26 lg CFU/g, and the value in control group sharply increased to 6.39 lg CFU/g on day 3, which suggested the pork was spoiled and inedible, while the TVC of the pork packaged with CS and CS/N-CDs films rose slowly with the values of 5.81 lg CFU/g on day 5 and 6.18 lg CFU/g on day 7, respectively, which signified the elevated microbial count in pork could be suppressed by CS and N-CDs. This was due to the antibacterial activities of CS and N-CDs that was effective for killing bacteria during storage and then decrease the TVC. Fig. 5E showed the change of a value of pork during storage. The gradually decreased a value was caused by the oxidization of non-stable oxymyoglobin (MbFeIIO2) and deoxyhemoglobin (MbFeII) into brownish metmyoglobin (MbFeIII). The pork packed with CS and CS/N-CDs films showed higher a value compared with that of the control, which was ascribe to the antioxidant activity of CS and N-CDs and oxygen barrier properties of CS and CS/N-CDs films. Also, the complex formed by the myoglobin and the compounds including H2S and H2O2 produced by microbes may cause discoloration. Both CS and N-CDs exhibited antibacterial activities against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Thus, pork in CS/N-CDs groups maintained relatively higher a value. As shown in Fig. 5F, the weight loss of pork gradually increased during the 9 days. It is noteworthy that the weight loss in CS/N-CDs group was always lower than that of CS and control. Nutrient loss may account for this phenomenon. For any food, UV light is one of the critical factors governing its nutrient loss. N-CDs could absorb UV light that was illustrated in Fig. 2C and Fig. S3. Thus, the incorporation of N-CDs into CS film matrix could be beneficial for reducing nutrient loss by blocking a certain amount of UV light thereby decrease the weight loss. During the storage, pork will go through three stages: rigidity, autolysis, and spoilage. Its structure and hardness will vary in different stages. Therefore, the hardness can be used as an indicator for freshness. The hardness of pork was corelated with several factors including pH value, fat, protein, moisture content and collagen. Springiness refers to the extent to which the meat sample is restored to its original state after the first compression. As presented in Fig. 5G, downward trends of pork hardness and springiness with the extension of storage time were found in all groups. The decreased hardness and springiness were due to the decomposition of protein catalyzed by protease enzymes produced from microbial growth (Maqsood, Abushelaibi, Manheem, Al Rashedi, & Kadim, 2015). Pork packaged with CS/N-CDs films showed the highest hardness and springiness compared with that of control, which resulted from the presence of CS and N-CDs because they can effectively inhibit the growth of S. aureus and E. coli, as confirm in the antibacterial test.
The toxicity of the pork wrapped with CS/N-CDs films were evaluated using in vitro studies with a mouse embryonic fibroblast cell (NIH-3 T3) and hemolytic activity test with blood cells from mouse.

From Fig. 5H, it can be observed that the cell viability of all groups presented a high level on day 1 (＞97%) and on day 9 (＞93%), which indicated the pork packed with CS or CS/N-CDs films had good cytocompatibility to NIH-3 T3 cells at experimental doses. Moreover, the cell viability in each group significantly decreased after being stored for 9 days, which may be ascribed to the spoilage of pork. It can be seen in Fig. 5I, all treatment groups showed a very low hemolysis ratio (＜5%), which suggested that the pork wrapped with CS or CS/N-CDs films had good blood compatibility (Liu et al., 2020).

A pH fluorescent responsive CS/N-CDs film with pork freshness monitoring and maintaining was developed. The indicating effect on freshness was achieved by the change of pork pH induced by the production of TVB-N during protein decomposition. The maintaining effect on freshness was due to the following three reasons: 1) Antibacterial properties of CS and N-CDs were effective on suppressing the growth of bacteria, thereby improving physicochemical properties; 2) Improved antioxidant and oxygen barrier properties of CS/N-CDs films were helpful in delaying the change of a value; 3) UV barrier property was beneficial for reducing nutrition loss. Hydrogen bonds were formed among the components. The film surface was hydrophobic and has acceptable water barrier property for food packaging. The incorporation of N-CDs improved the mechanical properties, increased the redness and yellowness, while decreased the lightness of CS/N-CDs films. There was no statistically significant difference in L, a, b and ΔE value of the films when adjust to different pH, which will not affect consumers to view the product from the outside of packaging. In summary, CS/N-CDs film was promising to be as an intelligent and active food packaging film for pork freshness monitoring and maintaining.

Wanmei Lin: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Gengli Huang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Weixia Yang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shuhao Zeng: Validation. Xueli Luo: Methodology. Jihong Huang: Methodology. Zhonghong Li: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
